_meta.notes: Ch3 L10. ayaw, gusto kong, ayaw kong.

cards.body: Ayaw means 'don't want' or 'don't like.' The opposite of gusto.

example.note: Gusto ko = I want. Ayaw ko = I don't want. Clean opposites.

cards.body: To say what you WANT to do, add -ng to ko and then the verb.

example.note: Ko + ng = kong. 'I want to [verb].' The -ng links them together.

cards.body: Same pattern for what you DON'T want to do.

example.want: Gusto kong magluto. (I want to cook.)
example.dont_want: Ayaw kong magluto. (I don't want to cook.)
example.note: Same structure, just swap gusto for ayaw.

options: I don't want to eat sinigang.
cards.explanation: Ayaw kong kumain = I don't want to eat.

cards.source_text: You want to drink coffee. Say it:
cards.explanation: Gusto kong uminom ng kape = I want to drink coffee.
Ayaw kong magluto = I don't want to cook.

cards.source_text: Lola offers adobo. You're full but want to be polite.
options: Busog na po ako, Lola. Salamat po!
cards.explanation: Busog na po ako = I'm already full (respectful). Direct 'ayaw ko' to Lola would be too blunt.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: In Filipino culture, directly saying 'ayaw ko' to an elder offering food is considered rude. The polite dodge is 'Busog na po ako' (I'm already full) even if you aren't.
The -ng linker (kong, mong, nang) is one of the most important connectors in Tagalog. It glues words together into phrases.
'Gusto kong kumain ng adobo' is a complete sentence. Subject (implied: I), desire (gusto), action (kumain), object (adobo). You're building real sentences now.
